word_list.pronunciation: kɹˈɔs ðæt bɹˈɪdʒ wɛn ˌɛsbˈiː kˈʌm ɡˈɛt tʊ ɪt

senses.definition: to not worry about something beforehand and deal with it whenever it happens

examples.x: Crossing that bridge when we get to it is how we move forward now knowing we can handle whatever comes in the future.
Martin asked what we'd do if our new company couldn't find good staff when we needed to expand, and I said we'll cross that bridge when we come to it.
The job interview is a week away, so I'm not worried about it yet—I'll cross that bridge when I get to it.
We don't need to decide on our vacation plans now; let's cross that bridge when we get to it.

senses.definition: to deal with things as they occur instead of planning for them in advance

examples.x: In life, you just have to take every challenge as it comes—there's no instruction manual.
Just take each day as it comes and do what you feel is right.
I don't have a detailed plan for my vacation; I'll just take it as it comes and go with the flow.
Life is full of uncertainties, so I've learned to take each day as it comes and make the most of it.
Rather than stressing about what might happen, I prefer to take this project as it comes and tackle challenges as they arise.

senses.definition: to allow things to unfold naturally without excessive interference or control, embracing a relaxed and accepting attitude towards the outcome

examples.x: I know you're worried about the outcome, but sometimes you just have to let it roll and see what happens.
Instead of stressing over every little detail, why not take a step back and let it roll?
She decided to let it roll and trust that everything would work out in the end.
When faced with a difficult decision, it's sometimes best to let it roll and listen to your instincts.
Rather than micromanaging the project, the team leader encouraged her colleagues to let it roll and foster creativity.

senses.definition: a condition or method that falls in between two opposing and extreme choices

examples.x: I aim to strike a happy medium between playing entertaining football and getting results.
In life generally we should try to find the happy medium.
Let's hope that common sense prevails and they find a happy medium.
In a healthy relationship, both partners need to find a happy medium between their individual needs and desires.
When it comes to spending money, it's important to find a happy medium between saving for the future and enjoying the present.

senses.definition: a situation where multiple factors, responsibilities, etc. must be handled or managed

examples.x: He performed a political balancing act, making gestures towards the left as well as the right.
I found myself having to do a balancing act between work and family.
Being a working parent often feels like a constant balancing act between career and family.
Running a successful business requires a delicate balancing act between cost management and providing quality products or services.
As a student-athlete, she had to perform a challenging balancing act between training, academics, and social life.

word_list.pronunciation: baɪ ðə sˈiːt ʌv wˈʌnz pˈænts

senses.definition: in a way that solely relies on one's intuition or personal judgment rather than the necessary knowledge, tools, etc.

examples.x: We started a magazine by the seat of our pants, without any of the market testing that’s done today.
He managed to navigate through the complex project by the seat of his pants, relying on his past experience.
The entrepreneur had no formal business training but built a successful company by the seat of her pants, learning as she went along.
The comedian didn't have a script for the show, so he had to rely on his wit and comedic timing, performing by the seat of his pants.
The team faced unexpected challenges during the competition and had to adapt their strategy by the seat of their pants.

senses.definition: to follow one's intuition instead of rules or what others say

examples.x: After a few years in the detective game, you learn to follow your nose.
I don't worry too much about the future because I just follow my nose when I'm faced with a big decision.
I followed my nose and didn't buy this dress a month ago and now it's on sale for 40% off.
In creative endeavors, sometimes it's best to ignore the rules and follow your nose to discover something unique and original.

senses.definition: something with a mixture of the different qualities of two separate things

examples.x: The new restaurant offers a menu that is a halfway house between traditional Italian cuisine and modern fusion dishes.
His writing style can be described as a halfway house between literary fiction and commercial entertainment.
The political proposal aims to find a halfway house between stricter regulations and maintaining individual freedom.
The artist's work reflects a unique halfway house between abstract and representational art forms.
The company's new product is positioned as a halfway house between a smartphone and a tablet, offering features of both.

senses.definition: to come to an agreement with someone by granting some of their requests while they grant some of one's requests

examples.x: The buyers wanted to bring the price down from $15,000 to $10,000, so I offered to meet them halfway at $12,500.
They didn't comply with all our demands, but met us halfway on the more important points.
We can't give you everything you've requested, but we can meet you halfway.

senses.definition: without any preparations or prior plans

examples.x: I hadn't prepared a speech so I just said a few words off the cuff.
I'm just speaking off the cuff here—I haven't seen the results yet.
Mr Baker was speaking off the cuff when he made those suggestions.

word_list.pronunciation: ˈɔf ðə tˈɑːp ʌv wˈʌnz hˈɛd

senses.definition: from one's memory, without spending time to carefully consider or think

examples.x: It was the best I could think of off the top of my head.
John can list the capital cities of all 50 states off the top of his head.
Just off the top of my head, I’d say there were about 50.
When asked about the capital of a particular country, she gave an answer off the top of her head, without looking it up.
During the meeting, he provided an estimate off the top of his head for the project's completion time.

word_list.pronunciation: plˈʌk ˌɛstˌiːˈeɪtʃ ˌaʊɾəv ɔːɹ fɹʌmðɪ ɔːɹ θˈɪn ˈɛɹ

senses.definition: to quickly say something without thinking about it first, usually as a reply to something

examples.x: When asked about his retirement plans, he seemed to pluck a date out of thin air, saying he'd retire on the 27th of October.
The student couldn't answer the difficult math question but managed to pluck the correct solution out of the air at the last moment.
During the brainstorming session, she plucked a brilliant idea out of thin air that impressed everyone in the room.

word_list.pronunciation: ɡˈɪv ɔːɹ kˈʌt ˌɛsbˈiː ɐ bɹˈeɪk

senses.definition: to stop being harsh or strict with someone

examples.x: I know he made a mistake, but can we give him a break?
After a long and exhausting day, Jane asked her family to give her a break and let her relax in peace.
I know you made a mistake, but can you cut me a break and give me another chance?
The boss decided to cut John a break by allowing him to take a few extra days off after his exhausting work on the project.
Instead of getting a parking ticket, the kind traffic officer cut Sarah a break and let her off with a warning.

senses.definition: to not punish or criticize someone for their inappropriate or rude action or behavior

examples.x: I know you're upset, Sarah, but that doesn't give you a free pass to treat other people like garbage.
Sally's usually very well behaved, so I gave her a pass on this one.
The press as always gave him a free pass as with so many other issues.
I know he was late for the meeting, but he had a family emergency, so we should give him a pass this time.

senses.definition: to treat a person in a more gentle and caring way and be less harsh or strict with them

examples.x: I only lost by six points, but I think he was going easy on me since he's so much better.
She's had a tough day, so go easy on her and don't criticize her work too harshly.
He's going through a difficult time, so let's go easy on him and offer our support.

senses.definition: to not be so harsh, strict, or critical when dealing with someone

examples.x: Cutting him some slack won't make him behave more politely next time you know.
I’m not emotionally ready for chores just yet, so please give me some slack.
You know I never make requests like this, cut me some slack.
John's been dealing with a personal crisis lately, so let's cut him some slack and not push him too hard about the project deadlines.

senses.definition: to deal with a situation or problem in a way that is the easiest instead of proper or honorable

examples.x: As soon as things got difficult he took the easy way out.
I just took the easy way out and gave him some cash.
I'm not the type that takes the easy way out.
Instead of confronting the issue, he took the easy way out by quitting his job.
Mary decided to take the easy way out of her diet and indulged in a whole cake on her cheat day.

senses.definition: a soft treatment that does not require someone to face the consequences of their actions

examples.x: Generally speaking, the press has given Jacobson a free ride.
No one has seriously examined the president’s trade policy – he’s gotten a free ride so far.
He always gets a free ride at work because of his family connections.
The government shouldn't provide a free ride to companies that don't pay their fair share of taxes.
Getting good grades in school requires studying; there's no free ride to success.

senses.definition: to think and react quickly to something without prior thought

examples.x: I've always been good at thinking on my feet.
During the job interview, she had to think on her feet when asked an unexpected question about her weaknesses.
In a crisis, a good leader needs to be able to think on their feet and make decisions under pressure.
During the debate, he had to think on his feet to respond to his opponent's unexpected arguments.
As a teacher, being able to think on your feet is essential when dealing with unexpected questions or classroom disruptions.

senses.definition: to make decisions or take actions without prior planning or preparation, based on how the situation develops

examples.x: As you know, that element of the plan was set up rather late, and we're playing it by ear at the moment.
We'll just have to play it by ear until we can get something definite sorted out.
We don't have a fixed agenda for the team meeting; we'll play it by ear and address the most pressing issues first.
The manager decided to play it by ear when dealing with the unexpected challenges that arose during the project.
In situations like these, it's often better to play things by ear and adapt to the needs of the moment.

word_list.pronunciation: biː ˈɔːl θˈɪŋz tʊ ˈɔːl pˈiːpəl ɔːɹ mˈɛn

senses.definition: to continuously change one's attitudes or opinions in order to please people

examples.x: There is little point in being all things to all people at all times.
He's always trying to be all things to all people, and as a result, he often comes across as insincere.
You can't be all things to all men, so focus on being authentic and true to your values.
The marketing team attempted to create a product that was all things to all people, but it ended up lacking a clear target audience.
